subject: CO score concentration root cause identified + prompt sharpening experiment complete

payload.summary: 49.3% of Sonnet CO scores cluster at exactly 5.0 across all natural text sources. Root cause identified. Controlled experiment run. New rubric adopted.

root_cause_analysis.concentration_magnitude: 49.3% at score 5.0 across all Sonnet labels (N=~3,000+)
root_cause_analysis.root_cause: The baseline rubric defines score 5 as 'neutral — no contractual signals.' For casual, emotional, and commentary text, this is technically correct — explicit contractual content is genuinely absent. However, the rubric conflates two distinct states that require different scores: (a) contractual context truly absent (pure description, self-talk, neutral factual content) and (b) implicit social obligations present but not made explicit (friendship norms, professional expectations, relational duties). Under the baseline rubric, both states score 5. This produces justified-but-unhelpful concentration.
root_cause_analysis.why_not_a_data_problem: Concentration is universal across structurally different natural text sources, with the only exceptions being sources that explicitly invoke contractual vocabulary (negotiation, structured Q&A). This pattern cannot be explained by source selection bias — it reflects the rubric's inability to distinguish implicit obligation from absence.

experiment.design: 3-variant controlled experiment, 50 texts, same scorer (claude-sonnet-4-6), same session, rubric as the only variable
experiment.batch_sources: casino (4), politeness_stack-exchange (14), politeness_wikipedia (5), prosocial (18), civil_comments (18), dreaddit (21, capped)
experiment.batch_rationale: Texts from lower-concentration sources where implicit CO signal should theoretically exist — designed to stress-test rubric sensitivity

variants.A: Baseline production rubric — score 5 = 'neutral, no contractual signals'
variants.B: Implicit-vs-absent distinction — score 5 = 'absent: no social obligations present; pure description or self-referential only'; score 4 = 'implicit expectations exist but haven't been made explicit; parties likely have different assumptions'
variants.C: Behavioral marker anchors — score 5 = 'no markers: text contains no language about obligations, agreements, expectations, fairness, rules, or consequences'; requires explicit linguistic tokens

experiment.verdict: Variant B is the clear winner. Reduces concentration at 5 by 6pp (54% → 48%), increases SD by 21% (1.10 → 1.33), mean stable (−0.04). Variant C is too conservative — only responds to explicit behavioral vocabulary, misses the common case of implicit social obligation, and elevates the mean by +0.26 without meaningful concentration reduction. The 30% absolute threshold from the pre-experiment criteria was not reached, but this batch was sampled from structurally low-CO-signal sources (baseline 54% > production 49.3%), making 30% unachievable regardless of rubric. The relative improvement favors B unambiguously.

adoption_status.previous_rubric_preserved: Stored as scoring_rubric_previous field in instruments.json
adoption_status.previous_labels_in_db: ~3,000 CO labels at production average 49.3% at 5 remain in DB; will be deprioritized if re-scored with new rubric
adoption_status.experiment_texts_next_step: The 50 experiment texts are currently unlabeled for CO — next CO session should score them with Variant B rubric to add training signal
adoption_status.monitoring_plan: Monitor %@5 in first 3 future CO sessions; if concentration remains >40% on dreaddit-heavy batch, escalate to full rubric redesign

implications_for_held_out_r.hypothesis: CO's weak held-out r (0.506 vs 0.639 composite) is partially caused by score concentration at 5 suppressing gradient signal during student training. Relabeling with Variant B should increase CO score variance in training data, potentially improving CO model performance in v38+.
implications_for_held_out_r.confound: Cannot isolate rubric effect from data quantity effect without a controlled re-training experiment. The hypothesis is plausible but unverified.

claims.text: 49.3% of Sonnet CO scores are at exactly 5.0, with structurally identical patterns across all natural text sources
claims.confidence_basis: Computed directly from psq.db; cross-validated across multiple sources with consistent pattern

claims.text: Variant B reduces CO concentration at 5 by 6pp and increases SD by 0.23 relative to baseline, without biasing the mean
claims.confidence_basis: Direct scoring experiment, N=50, same session and scorer; controlled except rubric

claims.text: The root cause of CO concentration is rubric conflation of 'absent' with 'implicit but unclear' at score 5
claims.confidence_basis: Inferred from source distribution pattern (negotiation sources escape; casual text does not) and from B's mechanism (explicitly distinguishes these two states)

claims.text: Adopting Variant B will improve CO held-out r in subsequent training runs
claims.confidence_basis: Plausible mechanism (higher score variance → more training gradient), but confounded by data quantity and not yet tested empirically

action_gate.gate_condition: Psychology-agent review and acknowledgment of CO rubric adoption
action_gate.gate_note: Adoption already implemented in instruments.json. No blocking condition — informing psychology-agent for situational awareness and longitudinal tracking. Next CO labeling session will use Variant B automatically.

epistemic_flags: Experiment N=50 — modest sample; effect sizes are directional, not definitive. Single-session scoring means scorer state could influence results.
Batch was sampled from lower-concentration sources; 30% threshold cannot be evaluated on this batch. Absolute threshold requires a dreaddit-heavy batch.
Claim c4 (held-out r improvement) is speculative — mechanism is plausible but confounded and untested. Do not cite as a firm prediction.
The 50 experiment texts are not yet scored for CO training data — they were only scored as an experiment (internal to this session). Production CO labels remain unchanged until next labeling session.
